text.title: Fixed-Price vs T&M CR Pricing Playbook Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules cross the BA / procurement boundary and produce a defensible CR pricing decision in either contract regime.

rule.statement: Every CR is classified at intake by contract type before any sizing: fixed-price (FP) → "margin event, mechanism = addendum or absorb", T&M → "velocity event, mechanism = re-baseline burn-down". The classification is stamped at the top of the CR memo; sizing must not start until the classification is recorded.

rationale.source: Change requests behave radically differently under fixed-price vs T&M

rule.rationale: Skipping the regime check leads to BAs accidentally treating an FP change as a velocity update, which means the margin is silently eaten until the project goes red weeks later. Stamping the regime first prevents the category error.

rule.statement: Under fixed-price, CRs are bucketed by size against the remaining undrawn contingency: ≤ 25% of contingency → candidate for absorb; 25-75% → candidate for scope-swap (drop equivalent-value scope to make room); > 75% or contingency exhausted → mandatory paid addendum. The bucket determines the default action; the BA may override only with a written reason approved by the vendor sponsor.

rationale.source: When to absorb, when to negotiate addendum, how to price risk

rule.rationale: Absorb-or-addendum cannot be vibes-based; tying it to remaining contingency keeps the vendor solvent and the client relationship intact. The override clause acknowledges that exceptional cases exist but forces them to be written down.

rule.statement: Under T&M, no CR is "absorbed" — every CR triggers a re-baseline of the burn-down and a written impact memo to the client showing forecasted hours delta, revised end date, and whether the new total exceeds any cap (not-to-exceed clause). The BA explicitly flags any CR that pushes the engagement above 90% of NTE within the next two sprints.

rationale.source: T&M change behaviour: every CR is a velocity event

rule.rationale: Under T&M the client pays for the change automatically, so the failure mode is not margin loss but invisible cap-creep. The 90%-of-NTE alarm is what stops a polite series of small CRs from arriving at the contract ceiling without warning.

rule.statement: Residual risk is priced explicitly, not folded into the headline number. The CR memo carries a "risk addendum" line that names the uncertainty (e.g. third-party API completeness, regulator interpretation, data-volume guess) and applies a percentage uplift bounded by category: low-risk integration 5%, medium-risk regulatory or third-party 10-20%, high-risk data-quality or scope-fuzzy 20-40%. The buyer sees the uplift line by line.

rationale.source: How to price risk on a CR at the BA/procurement boundary

rule.rationale: Hidden risk loading destroys trust when delivery comes in under estimate (vendor looks padded) or over (client refuses to pay). Disclosed loading converts CR negotiation from theatre to grown-up conversation about who carries what risk.

rule.statement: The handoff to procurement is a single artefact (the CR options matrix) that contains: classification (FP vs T&M), bucket (absorb / swap / addendum / defer), three options with priced uplift and residual risk, and the recommended option with a one-paragraph rationale. The BA never sends procurement a chat log or a meeting recap as a substitute; the artefact is the contract surface.

rationale.source: Crosses BA / procurement boundary which currently has no bridge content

rule.rationale: Procurement people work from documents, not from BA narrative. A single artefact lets them sign or push back inside their own workflow; anything less stalls the addendum cycle and inflates the implementation lag.

example.title: A scope-fuzzy fixed-price CR midway through delivery
example.code:
Intake stamp: CR-019 contract_type: fixed-price remaining_contingency: €12k
Sizing: Option A (absorb): estimated cost €7k = 58% of contingency Option B (swap): drop "admin export to CSV" feature, gain €6k room, then absorb Option C (addendum): full paid CR €11k + 15% risk addendum = €12.65k
Bucket decision: Size at 58% of contingency → in the 25-75% band → scope-swap candidate. BA recommends Option B (swap) with override rationale: "client already uses manual CSV; loss is acceptable, preserves contingency for two upcoming regulator-driven CRs identified in last week's discovery."
Handoff to procurement: artefact = CR-019-options-matrix.pdf attachments: revised SOW with feature swap, signed by client sponsor
Result: Margin protected, contingency preserved, no addendum needed, buyer sees the exact trade and approves in 2 days instead of 2 weeks.
example.note: The regime check forced the right framing; the bucket forced the right default; the artefact handed procurement a signable document instead of a meeting summary.
